success_surface: Multi-sheet formula creation with cross-referencing logic
effective_behaviors: Inspect workbook structure programmatically before applying changes
Build formula strings as raw Python strings with proper Excel syntax
Use COUNTIF or MATCH for existence checks across multiple sheets instead of LOOKUP functions
Apply IFERROR wrapper around nested conditions for graceful failure handling
Use absolute cell references for fixed input cells in formulas
Reference other sheets using 'SheetName!Range' syntax within formula strings
Validate formula placement by reading back cell.value after assignment
Preserve original workbook integrity by saving to separate output path
Log execution results including formula text and affected cell values
evidence: Agent called wb.sheetnames to discover all available sheets before building formula
Used openpyxl.load_workbook() then accessed calc_sheet = wb['Calc'] directly
Constructed formula string with nested IF(COUNTIF(...)) pattern checking three supplier sheets sequentially
Wrapped entire expression with IFERROR to handle case where item number exists in no sheet
Referenced input cell as $A$10 (absolute reference) inside formula string
Set calc_sheet['B7'] = formula using standard A1 notation
Printed verification output confirming formula was stored correctly: print(f"Verification - B7 value after setting: {calc_sheet['B7'].value}")
Saved output to new path without overwriting source file
Logged both the formula text and result value for transparency

memory_items.title: Workbook Structure Discovery
memory_items.lesson: Always enumerate available sheet names and inspect relevant cell contents before constructing formulas
memory_items.generalization: Call wb.sheetnames and verify target cell values via wb.worksheet.cell().value before any write operations

memory_items.title: Cross-Sheet Formula Construction
memory_items.lesson: For multi-sheet existence checks, use COUNTIF/MATCH nested in IF statements rather than VLOOKUP
memory_items.generalization: Build formulas as raw strings with 'SheetName!ColumnRange' references; wrap with IFERROR for missing values

memory_items.title: Formula Cell Assignment & Validation
memory_items.lesson: Assign formula strings to cells using A1 notation, then immediately validate by reading back the value
memory_items.generalization: After worksheet[cell] = formula, confirm with print(worksheet[cell].value) to ensure correct storage

memory_items.title: Input Cell Referencing Strategy
memory_items.lesson: Use absolute references ($) for fixed input cells referenced within formulas
memory_items.generalization: For dynamic lookup inputs, use $row$column notation to prevent formula range drift on copy

edits.content:
## 6. Cross-Sheet Formula Patterns
- **Existence Checks**: When determining if a value exists across multiple sheets, nest COUNTIF or MATCH calls inside IF statements rather than using VLOOKUP
- **Sheets as Ranges**: Reference entire columns/rows in formulas using 'SheetName!A:A' or 'SheetName!A:B' syntax within formula strings
- **Robust Error Handling**: Wrap multi-condition formulas with IFERROR to gracefully return empty or default values when matches are not found
- **Input Anchoring**: Use absolute cell references ($A$10 style) for any input cell that the formula depends on to maintain correct referencing
- **Assignment Verification**: After setting a cell formula, always read back worksheet['cell'].value to confirm the formula string was stored correctly (not evaluated prematurely)
- **Discovery First**: Enumerate wb.sheetnames and preview key cell contents before constructing complex cross-sheet formulas
